pattern: Because the input is already sorted, the largest squares live at the two ends, so a two-pointer merge from both sides builds the squared-and-sorted output in linear time.
hints: Negatives become large positives when squared, so the smallest absolute values sit somewhere in the middle and the biggest at the ends.
Compare the squares of the two extreme elements and pull the larger one to the back of the result, then shrink that side.

Use a left pointer at 0 and a right pointer at n-1; fill an output array from the last slot toward the first, always taking the bigger of the two squared endpoints and moving the pointer it came from.

edge_inputs.description: mix of negatives, zero, and positives
all negative, descending in magnitude after squaring
symmetric values around zero collapse to duplicates

stress.description: random sorted array spanning negatives and positives
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = sorted(rng.randint(-10000, 10000) for _ in range(n))
    return [nums]
stress.note: sorted by construction to satisfy the precondition

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def sortedSquares(self, nums):
        n = len(nums)
        res = [0] * n
        l, r = 0, n - 1
        pos = n - 1
        while l <= r:
            a = nums[l] * nums[l]
            b = nums[r] * nums[r]
            if a > b:
                res[pos] = a
                l += 1
            else:
                res[pos] = b
                r -= 1
            pos -= 1
        return res
